subject: Physics
options: suffer total internal reflection in both cases (A) and (B)
suffer total internal reflection in case (B) only
have partial reflection and partial transmission in case (B)
solution:
Velocity of light in medium
$$
\mathrm{V}_{\text {med }}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~cm}}{0.2 \mathrm{~ns}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}}{0.2 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~s}}=1.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
$$
Refractive index of the medium
$$
\mu=\frac{V_{\text {air }}}{V_{\text {med }}}=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
$$
As $\mu=\frac{1}{\sin C}$
$$
\therefore \sin \mathrm{C}=\frac{1}{\mu}=\frac{1}{2}=30^{\circ}
$$
Condition of TIR is angle of incidence i must be greater than critical angle. Hence ray will suffer TIR in case of $(B)\left(i=40^{\circ}>30^{\circ}\right)$ only.

⇒feff=-10 cm

subject: Physics
solution: To obtain final image at infinity, object which is the image formed by objective should be at focal distance of eye-piece. By lens formula (for objective) $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{v_0}-\frac{1}{u_0}=\frac{1}{f_0} \\ \text { or, } \quad & \frac{1}{v_0}-\frac{1}{-25}=\frac{1}{20} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{1}{v_0}=\frac{1}{20}-\frac{1}{25}=\frac{5-4}{100}=\frac{1}{100} \mathrm{~mm} \\ \therefore \quad & v_0=100 \mathrm{~mm} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore the distance between the lenses $$ =v_0+f_e=100 \mathrm{~mm}+20 \mathrm{~mm}=120 \mathrm{~mm} $$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given: $\mathrm{f}_0=50 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{e}}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mathrm{d}=25 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{u}_0=-200 \mathrm{~cm}$
Magnification $\mathrm{M}=$ ?
As $\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_0}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}_0}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_0}$
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}_0}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_0}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}_0}=\frac{1}{50}-\frac{1}{200}=\frac{4-1}{200}=\frac{3}{200}
$$
or $\mathrm{v}_0=\frac{200}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
Now $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{d}=-25 \mathrm{~cm}$
From, $\frac{1}{v_e}-\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& -\frac{1}{u_e}=\frac{1}{f_e}-\frac{1}{v_e} \\
& =\frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{25}=\frac{6}{25}
\end{aligned}
$$
or, $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}=\frac{-25}{6} \mathrm{~cm}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Magnification } \mathrm{M}=\mathrm{M}_0 \times \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{e}} \\
& =\frac{\mathrm{v}_0}{\mathrm{u}_0} \times \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}}{\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{e}}}=\frac{-200 / 3}{200} \times \frac{-25}{-25 / 6} \\
& =-\frac{1}{3} \times 6=-2
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
options: between the focus $(F)$ and the centre of curvature $(C)$ of the mirror ONLY
between the pole($P$) and the focus $(F)$ of the concave mirror ONLY
beyond the centre of the curvature $(C)$ of the mirror ONLY
at any point beyond the focus $(F)$ of the mirror
solution:
In the parallax method for determining the focal length of a concave mirror, we use an object pin and its real image formed by the mirror.
The method of 'no parallax' involves aligning the tip of the object pin with the tip of its real image. This is only possible if the image formed is real.
For a concave mirror, a real image is formed only when the object is placed at any position beyond the focus ($F$), i.e., $u > f$.
If the object is placed between the pole ($P$) and the focus ($F$), the image formed is virtual, and it cannot be caught on a screen or used for the standard parallax alignment with a physical pin in front of the mirror.
Therefore, the object should be placed at any point beyond the focus ($F$) of the mirror to ensure a real image is formed.

subject: Physics
solution:
For a mirror with magnification m = -3 (real image), object and image distances satisfy |u| + |v| = 40 cm.
Since m = v/u = -3, we have v = -3u, giving u + 3u = 40, so u = 10 cm and v = -30 cm.
Using mirror formula: $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{-30} = \frac{3-1}{30} = \frac{1}{15}$
Hence f = -15 cm.

subject: Physics
solution:
The magnification of a thin lens is given by $m = \frac{f}{f + u}$.
Given that the size of the images is equal for two different object positions, the magnitudes of the magnifications must be equal: $|m_1| = |m_2|$.
For a single lens to produce images of the same size at two different object distances, one image must be real and the other must be virtual.
Let $u_1 = -8$ cm and $u_2 = -24$ cm.
Then, $m_1 = -m_2$ (since one is real and one is virtual).
$\frac{f}{f + u_1} = -\frac{f}{f + u_2}$
$\frac{1}{f - 8} = -\frac{1}{f - 24}$
$f - 24 = -(f - 8)$
$f - 24 = -f + 8$
$2f = 32$
$f = 16$ cm.
The focal length of the lens is 16 cm.

subject: Physics
solution:
The optical path between any two points is proportional to the time of travel.

The distance traversed by light in a medium of refractive index μ in time t is given by d=vt .......(i)

Where v is velocity of light in the medium. The distance traversed by light in a vacuum in this time,

s=ct

=c×dv   [from equation (i)]

=dcv=μd .....(ii) (Sinceμ=cv)

This distance is the equivalent distance in vacuum and is called optical path.

Here, optical path for first ray =n1L1

Optical path for second ray =n2L2

Path difference =n1L1-n2L2

Now, phase difference =2πλ×path difference

=2πλ×n1L1-n2L2

solution:
From the above figure, i2=30°,

Now, 

∠OBA+∠ABR+∠RBP=180°⇒2∠OBA+60°=180°⇒∠OBA=60°

In triangle OAB,

∠OBA+∠AOB+∠OAB=180°⇒60°+75°+∠OAB=180°⇒∠OAB=45°

And,

∠OAB+∠BAI+∠IAQ=180°⇒2∠OAB+2i1=180°⇒i1=45°

Now the total deviation of the ray will be,

δ=360°-2i1+i2⇒δ=360°-230°+45°δ=210°

solution:
For case (1)
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1.5}{v_1}-\frac{1}{\propto}=\frac{(1.5-1)}{R_1} \\ & \frac{1}{f_1}-\frac{1.5}{v_1}=\frac{1-1.5}{\propto}\end{aligned}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{f_1}=\frac{(1.5-1)}{R_1}$ ...(1) $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{f_1}=\frac{1}{2 R_1}$ For case (2) $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1.5}{v_1}-\frac{1.2}{\infty}=\frac{(1.5-1.2)}{R_2} \\ & \frac{1.2}{f_2}-\frac{1.5}{v_1}=\frac{(1.2-1.5)}{\propto} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1.2}{f_2}=\frac{0.3}{R_2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{1}{4 R_2} \end{aligned}$ So clearly, $\frac{f_1}{f_2}=\frac{2 \times 2}{4 \times 3}=\frac{1}{3}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Assuming that the right angled prism is an isoceles prism, so the other angles will be 45° each.

⇒ Each incident ray will make an angle of 45° with the normal at face PR.

⇒ The wavelength corresponding to which the incidence angle is less than the critical angle, will pass through PR.

⇒θC= critical angle

⇒θC=sin-11μ

⇒ If θC≥45°

the light ray will pass

⇒θCRed=sin-111.27=51.94°

Red will pass.

⇒θCGreen=sin-111.42=44.76°

Green will not pass

⇒θCBlue=sin-111.49=42.15°

Blue will not pass

⇒ So only red will pass through PR.

subject: Physics
solution:
Given an equilateral prism, the angle of the prism is $A = 60^{\circ}$.
The refractive index of the prism is $\mu = \sqrt{2}$.
The emergent ray grazes the second surface, which means the angle of emergence is $e = 90^{\circ}$.
Applying Snell's law at the second surface: $\mu \sin(r_2) = 1 \cdot \sin(e)$.
$\sqrt{2} \sin(r_2) = \sin(90^{\circ}) = 1$.
$\sin(r_2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, which gives $r_2 = 45^{\circ}$.
For a prism, we know the relation $r_1 + r_2 = A$.
Substituting the values: $r_1 + 45^{\circ} = 60^{\circ}$.
$r_1 = 60^{\circ} - 45^{\circ} = 15^{\circ}$.
The angle of refraction at the incident surface is $r_1 = 15^{\circ}$.

subject: Physics
solution:
For light to come out through face 'AC', total internal reflection must not take place.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { i.e., } \theta < c \Rightarrow \sin \theta < \sin c \\
& \Rightarrow \sin \theta < \frac{1}{\mu}
\end{aligned}
$$
or $\quad \mu < \frac{1}{\sin \theta} \Rightarrow \mu < \frac{1}{\sin 45^{\circ}}$
$$
\Rightarrow \mu < \sqrt{2} \Rightarrow \mu < 1.414
$$
